entry.title: Notes for Principles of Mathematical Analysis (Walter Rudin)
entry.content:
Chapter 1 The Real and Complex Number Systems

Introduction

1.1 Example

Equation $p^2=2$ is not satisfied by any rational $p$ .

Let $A$ be the set of all positive rationals $p$, $s.t.$ $p^2<2$.

Let $B$ be the set of all positive rationals $p$, $s.t.$ $p^2>2$.

And $A$ has no largest number while $B$ has no smallest number.

Let

then,

For each $p$ in $A$, $q>p$ and $q$ is in $A$ .

For each $p$ in $B$, $q<p$ and $q$ is in $B$ .

1.2 Remark

The rational number system has certain gaps and the real number system will fill them.

1.3 Definition

  • $x\in A$, $x$ is a member of $A$.
  • $x otin A$, $x$ is not a member of $A$.
  • $\varnothing$, empty set
  • $A\subset B$

1.4 Definition

$\mathbb{Q}$, the rational number.

Ordered Sets

1.5 Definition

Let $S$ be a set. An order on $S$ is a relation, denoted by $<$, with the following two properties:

  1. If $x\in S$ and $y\in S$ then one and only one of the statements

    is true.

  2. If $x,y,z\in S$, if $x<y$ and $y<z$, then $x<z$.

Notation: $<, >, =, \leqslant, \geqslant$.

Negation of $x<y$ is $x\geqslant y$.

1.6 Definition

An ordered set is a set is a set $S$ in which an order is defined.

1.7 Definition

Suppose $S$ is an ordered set , and $E\subset S$. If $\exists eta\in S, s.t. x\leqslant eta$ for every $x\in E$, we say that $E$ is bounded above, and call $ eta$ an upper bound of $E$.

Lower bounds can be defined in the same way.

1.8 Definition

Suppose $S$ is an ordered set, $E\subset S$, and $E$ is bounded above. Suppose $\exists \alpha\in S$ with the following properties:

​ (a) $\alpha$ is an upper bound of $E$.

​ (b) If $\gamma<\alpha$ then $\gamma$ is not an upper bound of $E$.

​ (b’) (equivalent form) $ orall eta<\alpha,\exists\gamma\in E,s.t. eta<\gamma$.

Then $\alpha$ is called the least upper bound of $E$, or the supremum of $E$.

We denote,

The greatest lower bound, or infimum of $E$ (which is bounded below) is defined in the same manner.

1.9 Examples

(a) Sets $A$ and $B$ in 1.1

$A$ has no supremum in $\mathbb{Q}$.

$B$ has no infimum in $\mathbb{Q}$.

(b) $\alpha=\sup E$, but $\alpha$ may or may not be a member of $E$.

(c) $E=\{ 1/n |n\in{ \mathbb{Z}_{+} } \}$, $\inf E=0$, but $0 otin E$.

1.10 Definition

An ordered set $S$ is said to have the least-upper-bound property if the following is true:

If $E\subset S$, $E$ is not empty, and $E$ is bounded above, then $\sup E$ exists in $S$.

$\mathbb{Q}$ does not have the least-upper-bound property.

1.11 Theorem

Suppose $S$ is an ordered set with the least-upper-bounded property, $B\subset S$, $B$ is not empty, and $B$ is bounded below. Let $L$ be the set of all lower bounds of $B$.Then

exists in $S$, and $\alpha = \inf B$.

In particular, $\inf B$ exists in $S$.

Proof, For $B$ is bounded below,$L eq\varnothing$. $ orall x\in L, y\in B$ satisfy that $x\leqslant y$. And each $y\in B$ is a upper bounder of $L$. Thus, $L$ has a supremum $\alpha = \sup L$ in $S$.

$ orall\gamma < \alpha, \gamma\leqslant y( orall y\in B)$, thus $\alpha \leqslant y( orall y\in B)$ and $\alpha$ is a lower bound of $B, i.e. \alpha \in L$. Since $\alpha$ is an upper bound of $L$, $ orall eta>\alpha$, $ eta otin L$(Set of lower bounds). Hence, $\alpha$ is the infimum of $B$.

Field

1.12 Definition

A field is a set $F$ with two operations, called addition and multiplication, which satisfy the following so-called “field axioms”(A),(M) and (D):

(A) Axioms of addition

(A1) If $x\in F$ and $y\in F$, then their sum $x+y\in F$.

(A2) Addition is commutative: $ orall x,y\in F, x+y=y+x$.

(A3) Addition is associative: $ orall x,y,z\in F, (x+y)+z=x+(y+z)$.

(A4) $F$ contains an element $0, s.t. orall x\in F\quad 0+x=x$.

(A5) $ orall x \in F$ corresponds an element $-x\in F, s.t.\quad x+(-x)=0$.

(M) Axioms of multiplication

(M1) If $x\in F$ and $y \in F$, then their product $xy\in F$.

(M2) Multiplication is commutative: $ orall x,y \in F,xy =yx$.

(M3) Multiplication is associative: $ orall x,y,z\in F, (xy)z=x(yz)$.

(M4) $F$ contains an element $1 eq 0, s.t. orall x \in F,\quad 1x=x$.

(M5) If $x\in F$ and $x eq 0$ then $\exists 1/x\in F, s.t. \quad x(1/x)=1$.

(D) The distribution law

holds for all $x,y\in F$.

1.13 Remarks

(a) Some notations: $x-y,\dfrac{x}{y},x+y+z,xyz,x^2,x^3,2x,3x,\ldots$

(b) $\mathbb{Q}$ is a field.

(c) Properties from field axioms are familiar in rational numbers, real numbers and complex numbers.

1.14 Proposition

The axioms for addition imply the following statements.

(a) If $x+y=x+z$ then $y=z$. (Cancellation law)

(b) If $x+y=x$ then $y=0$.

(c) If $x+y=0$ then $y=-x$.

(d) $-(-x)=x$.

Proof, Omitted.

1.15 Proposition

The axioms for multiplication imply the following statements.

(a) If $x eq 0$ and $xy=xz$ then $y=z$.

(b) If $x eq 0$ and $xy=x$ then $y=1$.

(c) If $x eq 0$ and $xy=1$ then $y=1/x$.

(d) If $x eq 0$ then $1/(1/x)=x$.

Proof, Omitted.

1.16 Proposition

The field axioms imply the following statements, for any $ x,y,z\in F$.

(a) $0x=0$.

(b) If $x eq 0$ and $y eq 0$ then $xy eq 0$.

(c) $(-x)y=-(xy)=x(-y)$.

(d) $(-x)(-y)=xy$.

Proof, Omitted.

1.17 Definition

An ordered field is a field $F$ which is also an ordered set, such that

(a) $x+y<x+z$, if $x,y,z\in F$ and $y<z$.

(b) $xy>0$, if $x\in F, y\in F,x>0,y>0$.

If $x>0(x<0)$, we call $x$ positive(negative).1

1.18 Proposition

The following statements are true in every ordered field.

(a) If $x>0$ then $-x<0$ and vice versa.

(b) If $x>0$ and $y< z$ then $ xy< xz $.

(c) If $x<0$ and $y< z$ then $ xy>xz $.

(d) If $x eq0$ then $x^2>0$. In particular, $1>0$.

(e) If $0<x<y$ then $0<1/y<1/x$.

Proof, Omitted.

The Real Field

1.19 Theorem

There exists an ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ which has the least-upper-bound property. Moreover, $\mathbb{R}$ contains $\mathbb{Q}$ as a subfield ($ \mathbb{Q}\subset\mathbb{R} $).

Proof, See the Appendix to Chap.1.

1.20 Theorem

(a) $ orall x,y\in\mathbb{R},x>0, \exists n\in\mathbb{N},s.t. nx>y$.(Archimedean property)

(b) $ orall x,y\in\mathbb{R}, x<y, \exists p\in\mathbb{Q}, s.t. x<p<y$.($\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$)

Proof, (a) Let $A=\left\{nx|x\in\mathbb{R},n\in\mathbb{N} ight\}$. If (a) is false, then $y$ would be an upper bound of $A$. Thus, suppose $\alpha=\sup A$. Since $\alpha-x<\alpha$, $\exists n_0\in\mathbb{N}, s.t. \alpha-x<n_0x<\alpha$. Thus, $\alpha<(n_0+1)x$, contradicted to that $A$ is upper-bounded.

(b) From (a) we obtain a positive integer $n$ such that,

Apply (a) again, positive integers $m_1$ and $m_2$ are obtained such that $m_1>nx$ and $m_2>-nx$. Then,

Hence, $\exists m\in \mathbb{N},-m_2\leqslant m\leqslant m_1$, such that,

Then, combine these inequalities,

Since $n>0$,

1.21 Theorem

$ orall x\in\mathbb{R}_+, orall n\in\mathbb{Z}_+,\exists ! y\in \mathbb{R}_+,s.t. y^n=x$.

This number $y$ is written $\sqrt[n]{x}$ or $x^{1/n}$.

Sketch of Proof, Let $E=\left\{t|t^n<x ight\}$, and $y=\sup E$. And find contradictions when $y^n eq x$.

Choose $h<\dfrac{x-y^n}{n(y+1)^{n-1}}$,

Try to prove that when $y^n<x$, $y+h\in E$ but $y<y+h$.

Choose $k=\dfrac{x-y^n}{ny^{n-1}}$,

Try to prove that when $y^n>x$, $y-k$ is an upper bound of $E$ but $y-k<y$.

Corollary

$ orall a,b\in\mathbb{R}_+, n\in\mathbb{N}_+, (ab)^{1/n}=a^{1/n}b^{1/n}$.

Proof, Hint: Let$\alpha = a^{1/n}, eta=b^{1/n}$, and use Thm. 1.21.

1.22 Decimal

Suppose $x\in\mathbb{R}_+$. Then choose integer $n_0$ such that $n_0\leqslant x$. Similarly choose integers $n_1,n_2,\ldots,n_k$ such that

Let $E$ be the set of these numbers

Then $x=\sup E$. The decimal expansion of $x$ is

The Extended Real Number System

1.23 Definition

The extended real number system $\overline{\mathbb{R}}=\mathbb{R}\cup{\pm\infty}$ .

And $ orall x\in\mathbb{R}, -\infty<x<+\infty$.

$\overline{\mathbb{R}}$ is not a field.

$+\infty$ is an upper bound of $\overline{\mathbb{R}}$.

(a) $ orall x\in\mathbb{R}$,

(b) $ orall x\in\mathbb{R}_+, x\cdot(+\infty)=+\infty, x\cdot(-\infty)=-\infty$.

(c) $ orall x\in\mathbb{R}_-, x\cdot(+\infty)=-\infty, x\cdot(-\infty)=+\infty$.

The Complex Field

1.24 Definition

A Complex number is an ordered pair $(a,b)$ of real numbers.

Let $x=(a,b),y=(c,d)$, and $x=y$ if and only if $a=c,b=d$.

Addition is defined as $x+y=(a+c,b+d)$.

Multiplication is defined as $xy=(ac-bd,ad+bc)$.

1.25 Theorem

These definitions of addition and multiplication turn the set of all complex numbers into a field, with $(0,0)$ and $(1,0)$ in the role of $0$ and $1$.

Proof, Verify the field axioms.

1.26 Theorem

$ orall a,b\in\mathbb{R},(a,0)+(b,0)=(a+b,0),\quad (a,0)(b,0)=(ab,0)$.

Proof, The proof is trivial.

1.27 Definition

$i=(0,1)$.

1.28 Theorem

$i^2=-1$.

Proof, The proof is trivial.

1.29 Theorem

$ orall a,b\in\mathbb{R},(a,b)=a+bi$.

Proof, The proof is trivial.

1.30 Definition

$z=a+bi\in\mathbb{C}$

The conjugate of $z$ is $ ar{z}=a-bi$.

The real part of $z$ is $a=Re(z)$.

The imaginary part of $z$ is $b=Im(z)$.

1.31 Theorem

$ orall z,w\in\mathbb{C}$,

(a) $\overline{z+w}=\overline{z}+\overline{w}$.

(b) $\overline{zw}= ar{z} ar{w}$

(c) $z+ ar{z}=2Re(z),z- ar{z}=2Im(z)$.

(d) $z ar{z}$ is real and non-negative.

Proof, Omitted.

1.32 Definition

$z\in \mathbb{C}$, the absolute value of $z$ is $|z|=(z ar{z})^{1/2}$.

1.33 Theorem

$z,w\in\mathbb{C}$,

(a) $|z|>0$ if $z=0$, $|z|=0$ if $z=0$.

(b) $| ar{z}|=|z|$.

(c) $|zw|=|z||w|$.

(d) $|Re(z)|\leqslant|z|$.

(e) $|z+w|\leqslant|z|+|w|$.

Proof, Omitted.

1.34 Notation

$x_1,\ldots,x_n\in\mathbb{C}$,

$\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n}x_j=x_1+\ldots+x_n$.

1.35 Theorem

(Schwarz inequality)

If $a_1,\ldots,a_n$ and $b_1,\ldots,b_n$ are complex numbers, then

Proof, Hint: use Lagrange’s identity.

Euclidean Spaces

1.36 Definition

For each positive integer $k$, let $\mathbb{R}^k$ be the set of all ordered $k$-tuples, $\mathbf{x}=(x_1,\ldots,x_k)$, where $x_1,\ldots,x_k\in \mathbb{R}$ is called coordinate of $\mathbf{x}$. The elements in $\mathbb{R}^k$ are called points. If $\mathbf{y}=(y_1,\ldots,y_k)$ and if $\alpha$ is a real number, put

so that $\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{y}\in\mathbb{R}^k$ and $\alpha\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^k$. $\mathbb{R}^k$ is a vector space over the real field. The zero element of $\mathbb{R}^k$ is $\mathbf{0}=(0,\ldots,0)$.

Inner product(scalar product), $\mathbf{x}\cdot\mathbf{y}=\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^k x_i y_i$.

The norm of $\mathbf{x}$, $|\mathbf{x}|=(\mathbf{x}\cdot\mathbf{x})^{1/2}=\left(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^k x_i^2 ight)^{1/2}$.

The structure defined is called Euclidean $k$-space.

1.37 Theorem

$\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y},\mathbf{z}\in\mathbb{R}^k$, and $\alpha$ is real. Then,

(a) $|\mathbf{x}|\geqslant 0$.

(b) $|\mathbb{x}=0|=0 \Leftrightarrow \mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$.

(c) $|\alpha\mathbf{x}|=|\alpha||\mathbf{x}|$.

(d) $|\mathbf{x}\cdot\mathbf{y}|\leqslant|\mathbf{x}||\mathbf{y}|$.

(e) $|\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{y}|\leqslant|\mathbf{x}|+|\mathbf{y}|$.

(f) $|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{z}|\leqslant|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}|+|\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{z}|$.

Proof, Omitted.

1.38 Remarks

$\mathbb{R}^k$ can be regarded as a metric space.

Appendix

Theorem 1.19 will be proved in this appendix by constructing $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$.

We denote rational numbers with English letters $p,q,r,\ldots$,while Greek letters $\alpha, eta,\gamma,\ldots$, are used to denote the cuts.

This method used here were invented by Dedekind.

(Cantor constructed $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$ by means of Cauchy sequences.)

Step 1

The members of $\mathbb{R}$ will be certain subsets of $\mathbb{Q}$, called cuts.

Definition of a cut: any set $\alpha\subset\mathbb{Q}$ with the following properties.

  1. $\alpha$ is not empty, and $\alpha eq\mathbb{Q}$.
  2. If $p\in\alpha,q\in\mathbb{Q}$, and $q<p$, then $q\in\alpha$.
  3. If $p\in\alpha$, then $p<r$ for some $r\in\alpha$.

Some facts:

  • $\alpha$ has no largest member.
  • If $p\in\alpha, q otin\alpha$ then $p<q$.
  • If $r\in\alpha, r<s$ then $s otin\alpha$.

Step 2

Define ‘’$\alpha< eta$’’ to mean: $\alpha\subsetneq eta$.

This meets the requirement of Def. 1.5.(Why?)

And only one in three relations can hold for any pair $\alpha, eta$.

$\mathbb{R}$ is an ordered set.

Step 3

The ordered set $\mathbb{R}$ has the least-upper-bound property.

Let $A\subset\mathbb{R},A eq\varnothing$, and assume that $ eta\in\mathbb{R}$ is an upper bound of $A$.

Define $\gamma$ to be the union of all $\alpha\in A$. ( If and only if $ p\in\alpha$ for some $\alpha\in A$)

  1. Verify that $\gamma$ meets the properties in Step 1, then $\gamma\in\mathbb{R}$.
  2. Verify that $\gamma=\sup A$ (Def. 1.8).

Step 4

Axioms of Addition

If $\alpha, eta\in\mathbb{R}$, we define $\alpha+ eta$ to be the set of all sums $r+s$, where $r\in\alpha,s\in eta$.

$0^$ is defined to be the set of all negative rational numbers.($0^$ is a cut.)

Please Verify that the axioms of addition (see Def. 1.12) hold in $\mathbb{R}$, with $0^*$ playing the role of $0$.

Step 5

(a) in Def. 1.17 can be proved.

Prop. 1.14 is valid in $\mathbb{R}$.

Step 6

Multiplication

Define $\mathbb{R}_+$ to be the set of all $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$ with $\alpha>0^*$.

$\alpha, eta\in\mathbb{R}_+$, $\alpha eta$ is define to be the set of all $p$ such that $p\leqslant rs$ for some choice of $r\in\alpha,s\in eta,r>0,s>0$.

$1^*$ is defined to be the set of all $q<1$.

Then the axioms (M) and (D) in Def. 1.12 hold in $\mathbb{R}_+$ with $1^*$ in the role of $1$.

(b) in Def. 1.17 holds now.

Step 7

Complete the definition of multiplication

Set $ \alpha 0^* = 0^* \alpha=0^* $.

Set

By the identity $-(-\gamma)=\gamma$, we can prove that the axioms (M) hold in $\mathbb{R}$.

Try to prove distribution law (D) in $\mathbb{R}$. (Break it into cases.)

Till now, $\mathbb{R}$ is an ordered field with the least-upper-bound property.

Step 8

$r^*$ is associated with $r\in\mathbb{Q}$, which is defined to be the set of all $p\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that $p<r$.

$r^*$ is a cut and the following relations are satisfied,

(a) $r^* +s^* =(r+s)^*$.

(b) $r^* s^* =(rs)^*$.

(c) $ r^* <s^* $ if and only if $r< s $.

Here we prove (a).

Choose $p\in r^* + s^* $. Then $p=u+v$, where $u< r,v < s$. Hence $p< r+s$, which says that $p\in(r+s)^*$.

Conversely, suppose $p\in(r+s)^*$. Then $p<r+s$. Choose $t$ so that $2t=r+s-p$, put

Then $ r^{\prime}\in r^* , s^{\prime}\in s^* $, and $p=r^\prime+s^\prime$, so that $p\in r^* +s*$.

Try to prove (b),(c).

Step 9

The ordered field $\mathbb{Q}$ is isomorphic to the order field $\mathbb{Q}^*$ we establish with the method in Step 8.

This identification of $\mathbb{Q}$ with $\mathbb{Q}^*$ allows us to regard $\mathbb{Q}$ as a subfield of $\mathbb{R}$.

The fact below holds,

Any two ordered field with the least-upper-bound property are isomorphic

Exercises

Q5. Let $A$ be a nonempty set of real numbers which is bounded below. Let $-A$ be the set of all numbers $-x$, where $x\in A$. Prove that

Q6. Fix $b>1$.

(a) If $m,n,p,q$ are integers, $n>0,q>0$, and $r=m/n=p/q$, prove that

Hence it makes sense to define $b^r=(b^m)^{1/n}$.

(b) Prove that $b^{r+s}=b^rb^s$ if $r$ and $s$ are rational.

(c) If $x$ is real, define $B(x)$ to be the set of all numbers $b^t$, where $t$ is rational and $t\leqslant x$. Prove that

when $r$ is rational. Hence it makes sense to define

for every real $x$.

(d) Prove that $b^{x+y}=b^xb^y$ for all real $x$ and $y$.

Q7. Fix $b>1, y>0$, and prove that there is a unique real $x$ such that $b^x=y$, by completing the following outline. (This $x$ is called the logarithm of $y$ to the base $b$.)

(a) For any positive integers $n$, $b^n-1\geqslant n(b-1)$.

(b) Hence $b-1\geqslant n(b^{1/n}-1)$.

(c) If $t>1$ and $n>(b-1)/(t-1)$, then $b^{1/n}<t$.

(d) If $w$ is such that $b^w<y$, then $b^{w+(1/n)}<y$ for sufficiently large $n$; to see this, apply part (c) with $t=y\cdot b^{-w}$.

(e) If $b^w>y$, then $b^{w-(1/n)}>y$ for sufficiently large $n$.

(f) Let $A$ be the set of all $w$ such that $b^w<y$, and show that $x=\sup A$ satisfies $b^x=y$.

(g) Prove that this $x$ is unique.

Q20. With reference to the Appendix, suppose that property 3. were omitted from the definition of a cut. Keep the same definitions of order and addition. Show that the resulting ordered set has the least-upper-bound property, that addition satisfies axioms (A1) to (A4) (with a slightly different zero-element!) but that (A5) fails.
entry.summary: Chapter 1 The Real and Complex Number Systems
